proofs.proof_tex:
Fix a sufficiently small \(\delta
e0\), and abbreviate \(p=p_{+,\delta}\), \(q=p_{-,\delta}\). By thm:local-geometry, \(C_\star(\delta)>0\), \(C_m(\delta)>0\), and the mirror affinity is minimized at exponent \(1/2\). Hence
\[A:=e^{-C_\star(\delta)}=\int_{\mathbb R^2}\sqrt{pq}<1.	ag{13}\]
Define the tilted density and log likelihood ratio
\[m=A^{-1}\sqrt{pq},\qquad \ell=\log(p/q).	ag{14}\]
Both are well defined because the Gaussian-ANM densities are positive on all of \(\mathbb R^2\). Coordinate exchange leaves \(m\) invariant and sends \(\ell\) to \(-\ell\). Moreover \(E_m\ell^2<\infty\): with \(u=p/q\), the elementary bound \(\sqrt u(\log u)^2\le K(1+u)\), \(u>0\), gives
\[E_m\ell^2\le (K/A)\int q(1+p/q)=2K/A.\]
Thus \(E_m\ell=0\); write \(\sigma^2=E_m\ell^2\).

Let \(B_n(\delta)\) be the equal-prior Bayes error defined in lem:mirror-bayes-minimax-equality. For \(S_n=\sum_{i=1}^n\ell(X_i,Y_i)\) under \(m^n\), the identities \(p=Ame^{\ell/2}\) and \(q=Ame^{-\ell/2}\) imply
\[B_n(\delta)= rac{A^n}{2}E_{m^n}e^{-|S_n|/2}.	ag{15}\]
The expectation is at most one. If \(\sigma>0\), Chebyshev's inequality gives \(m^n\{|S_n|\le2\sigma\sqrt n\}\ge3/4\), and therefore
\[ rac38A^ne^{-\sigma\sqrt n}\le B_n(\delta)\le rac12A^n.	ag{16}\]
If \(\sigma=0\), then \(S_n=0\) \(m^n\)-almost surely and (16) holds with the evident sharper equality. Consequently
\[-n^{-1}\log B_n(\delta)\longrightarrow-\log A=C_\star(\delta).	ag{17}\]
The exact mirror equalizer identity in lem:mirror-bayes-minimax-equality gives \(R_n(\delta)=B_n(\delta)\) for every \(n\), including any likelihood-ratio ties. Combining this identity with (17) proves
\[R_n(\delta)=\exp\{-n[C_\star(\delta)+o_n(1)]\}.	ag{18}\]
For fixed \(\delta
e0\), division by the positive \(C_m(\delta)\) gives
\[\lim_{n	o\infty} rac{-\log R_n(\delta)}{nC_m(\delta)}= rac{C_\star(\delta)}{C_m(\delta)}.\]
Now take the outer limit \(\delta\downarrow0\). Thm:local-geometry yields \(C_\star(\delta)/C_m(\delta)	o c_2/(8c_1)\), where \(c_1,c_2>0\). This proves the stated iterated limit, with the order of limits explicitly fixed, and shows pointwise on the admissible mirror path that the sharp likelihood exponent is linear in the native score-variance gap.

theorem.statement: The answer is no. Along every sequence delta_n downarrow 0 with r_n=o(sqrt(C_m(delta_n))) and n C_m(delta_n)->infinity, the cross-fitted score-contrast template is sign consistent for every training-measurable nuisance collection satisfying ass:jacobian-estimability. Nevertheless that L4 O_P(r_n) premise alone cannot certify any positive fraction of the Chernoff exponent: for every such sequence and every admissible baseline nuisance collection, there is another training-measurable collection satisfying the identical uniform L4 O_P(r_n) condition whose corresponding detector has P_{+,delta_n}^n{D_n=-} >= exp{-2 sqrt(n C_m(delta_n))}, and hence [-log max_h P_{h,delta_n}^n{D_n != h}]/[n C_star(delta_n)]->0. Thus the premise proves consistency, including under the original stronger condition r_n=o(C_m(delta_n)), but exponential tail or stability control beyond convergence in probability is needed to infer Chernoff efficiency for an instantiated nuisance-training algorithm.
theorem.proof_tex:
Fix a sequence in the statement and put \(a_n=nC_m(\delta_n)	o\infty\). Lem:crossfitted-variance-sign-consistency-sharp proves, under the weaker requirement \(r_n=o(\sqrt{C_m(\delta_n)})\), that every admissible instantiation of the cross-fitted score contrast has maximal sign error tending to zero. In particular, this applies under the original question's stronger requirement \(r_n=o(C_m(\delta_n))\).

To test whether the same premise controls the exponential scale, start from any admissible baseline collection and apply lem:rare-event-l4-contamination. The modified collection remains training-measurable and satisfies exactly the same uniform \(L^4\;O_P(r_n)\) condition, but its maximal error \(e_n\) obeys
\[e_n:=\max_{h\in\{+,-\}}P_{h,\delta_n}^n(D_n
e h)\ge P_{+,\delta_n}^n(D_n=-)\ge e^{-2\sqrt{a_n}}.	ag{26}\]
By thm:local-geometry,
\[ rac{C_\star(\delta_n)}{C_m(\delta_n)}\longrightarrow\kappa:= rac{c_2}{8c_1}>0.	ag{27}\]
Since \(0<e_n\le1\), (26)--(27) yield
\[0\le rac{-\log e_n}{nC_\star(\delta_n)}\le rac{2\sqrt{a_n}}{a_n\{C_\star(\delta_n)/C_m(\delta_n)\}}\longrightarrow0.	ag{28}\]
Chernoff efficiency would instead require the ratio in (28) to converge to one, with thm:local-minimax-frontier identifying the likelihood benchmark. Hence the declared \(L^4\;O_P(r_n)\) premise does not imply the proposed exponent. There is no contradiction with consistency: the contaminating training event has probability tending to zero, so it is invisible to an \(O_P\) condition, yet it is exponentially much more likely than \(e^{-nC_\star(\delta_n)}\). This is an insufficiency result for the assumption class and the cross-fitted score-contrast template; it does not assert inefficiency of every concrete SCORE nuisance-training algorithm.

added_lemmas.statement: For n>=1 and delta in (-delta_0,delta_0), define the equal-prior Bayes error B_n(delta)=(1/2) inf_{pi in T_n}[P_{+,delta}^n{pi=-}+P_{-,delta}^n{pi=+}]=(1/2) integral min(p_{+,delta}^n,p_{-,delta}^n). Then the mirror experiment satisfies the exact finite-sample identity R_n(delta)=B_n(delta). A deterministic Borel likelihood-ratio rule attaining this identity exists even when the likelihood-ratio tie set has positive probability.
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
Let \(\Omega_n=(\mathbb R^2)^n\), let \(S:\Omega_n	o\Omega_n\) exchange the two coordinates of every observation, and write \(p_n=p_{+,\delta}^n\), \(q_n=p_{-,\delta}^n\). The mirror definition gives the pointwise identity \(q_n(\omega)=p_n(S\omega)\). The densities are positive and continuous because \(b=1-\alpha^2>0\) and \(g\in C^3\). Thus \(\Lambda(\omega)=\log\{p_n(\omega)/q_n(\omega)\}\) is Borel and satisfies \(\Lambda(S\omega)=-\Lambda(\omega)\).

The ordinary likelihood-ratio prescription chooses \(+\) on \(\{\Lambda>0\}\) and \(-\) on \(\{\Lambda<0\}\). On the tie set \(T=\{\Lambda=0\}\), locate the first index \(i\) with \(x_i
e y_i\), choose \(+\) if \(x_i>y_i\), and choose \(-\) otherwise. On the fixed set \(F=\{x_i=y_i	ext{ for every }i\}\), choose either label. This rule \(\pi^*\) is Borel. The set \(F\) has Lebesgue measure zero and hence probability zero under both positive densities, while
\[\pi^*(S\omega)=-\pi^*(\omega)\qquad(\omega
otin F).	ag{19}\]
The rule is Bayes optimal because it chooses a density maximizer pointwise, ties being immaterial to average risk. Therefore its equal-prior average error is \(B_n(\delta)=	frac12\int\min(p_n,q_n)\). Its two errors are equal: changing variables by the measure-preserving involution \(S\), using (19) and \(q_n(S\omega)=p_n(\omega)\), gives
\[P_{-,\delta}^n(\pi^*=+)=P_{+,\delta}^n(\pi^*=-).	ag{20}\]
Since their average is \(B_n(\delta)\), each error equals \(B_n(\delta)\), so \(R_n(\delta)\le B_n(\delta)\). Conversely, for every rule, maximum error is at least average error, and average error is at least the Bayes minimum \(B_n(\delta)\). Hence \(R_n(\delta)\ge B_n(\delta)\), proving equality. The same tie split works at \(\delta=0\), where the entire sample space is a likelihood tie up to null sets.

added_lemmas.statement: Let finitely many dominated one-dimensional paths Q_{r,t} be differentiable in quadratic mean at a common law Q_0 with tangent scores a_r in L2_0(Q_0). If t_n->0 and sqrt(n)t_n->tau, then jointly over r, under Q_0^n, log(dQ_{r,t_n}^n/dQ_0^n)=t_n sum_{i=1}^n a_r(W_i)-(n t_n^2/2)||a_r||_2^2+o_{Q_0^n}(1), and the vector of central sequences converges to a centered Gaussian vector with covariance tau^2 <a_r,a_s>.

source.locator: Theorem 7.2 (quadratic-mean differentiability implies the LAN likelihood expansion), with the finite joint version obtained by its simultaneous application and the multivariate central limit theorem

attestation.note: Stable DOI supplied for A. W. van der Vaart, Asymptotic Statistics (1998), Theorem 7.2; no verbatim transcription is claimed.

added_lemmas.statement: Let Q_{+,t} and Q_{-,t} be dominated paths differentiable in quadratic mean at a common Q_0 with tangent scores a_+,a_- in L2_0(Q_0), and suppose ||a_+||_2=||a_-||_2. Put d=a_+-a_-. If t_n->0 and sqrt(n)t_n->tau in (0,infinity), then Lambda_n=log(dQ_{+,t_n}^n/dQ_{-,t_n}^n) converges under Q_{+,t_n}^n to N(tau^2||d||_2^2/2,tau^2||d||_2^2) and under Q_{-,t_n}^n to N(-tau^2||d||_2^2/2,tau^2||d||_2^2). If sqrt(n)t_n->0, the total-variation distance between Q_{+,t_n}^n and Q_{-,t_n}^n tends to zero.
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
Write \(I=\|a_+\|_2^2=\|a_-\|_2^2\), \(K=\langle a_+,a_-angle\), and \(D=\|d\|_2^2=2(I-K)\). By lem:qmd-product-lan, under \(Q_0^n\), jointly for \(h\in\{+,-\}\),
\[L_{h,n}:=\log rac{dQ_{h,t_n}^n}{dQ_0^n}=t_n\sum_{i=1}^na_h(W_i)- rac12nt_n^2I+o_{Q_0^n}(1).	ag{21}\]
Let \((G_+,G_-)\) be centered Gaussian with \(\operatorname{Cov}(G_r,G_s)=\langle a_r,a_sangle\). Subtraction in (21) gives, under \(Q_0^n\),
\[(L_{+,n},L_{-,n},\Lambda_n)\Rightarrow(	au G_+-	frac12	au^2I,	au G_--	frac12	au^2I,	au(G_+-G_-)).	ag{22}\]
For each \(h\), \(e^{L_{h,n}}\) is the likelihood ratio of \(Q_{h,t_n}^n\) relative to \(Q_0^n\), so it has expectation one. Its limit in (22) is lognormal with expectation one as well. Nonnegativity, convergence in distribution, and convergence of first moments imply uniform integrability. Hence, for every bounded continuous \(f\), change of measure and (22) give
\[E_{Q_{h,t_n}^n}f(\Lambda_n)=E_{Q_0^n}[e^{L_{h,n}}f(\Lambda_n)]	o E[e^{	au G_h-	au^2I/2}f\{	au(G_+-G_-)\}].	ag{23}\]
Exponential tilting of a Gaussian vector preserves covariance and shifts the mean of \(	au(G_+-G_-)\) by its covariance with \(	au G_h\). For \(h=+\) this covariance is \(	au^2(I-K)=	au^2D/2\); for \(h=-\) it is \(	au^2(K-I)=-	au^2D/2\). Its variance is \(	au^2D\). Equation (23) therefore proves the two asserted normal limits.

If \(\sqrt n t_n	o0\), quadratic-mean differentiability gives
\[\|\sqrt{dQ_{+,t_n}/dQ_0}-\sqrt{dQ_{-,t_n}/dQ_0}\|_2^2=	frac14t_n^2D+o(t_n^2).\]
Thus the one-observation Hellinger affinity is \(1-O(t_n^2)\), and the product affinity is \(1-o(1)\) because \(nt_n^2	o0\). The standard inequality \(\|Q-R\|_{m TV}\le\sqrt{2\{1-\int\sqrt{dQdR}\}}\) then gives \(\|Q_{+,t_n}^n-Q_{-,t_n}^n\|_{m TV}	o0\).

added_lemmas.statement: For every sequence delta_n downarrow 0, if n C_m(delta_n)->rho in [0,infinity), then R_n(delta_n)->Phi(-(1/2)sqrt(rho c_2/c_1)), where Phi is the standard-normal distribution function. If n C_m(delta_n)->infinity, then R_n(delta_n)->0. These limits are pointwise for the fixed admissible mirror path.
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
Let \(d=v_+-v_-\). Thm:local-geometry proves quadratic-mean differentiability of both paths at \(P_0\) with tangents \(v_+,v_-\), and \(\|d\|_{L^2(P_0)}^2=c_2\). Coordinate exchange preserves \(P_0\) and maps \(v_+\) to \(v_-\), so \(\|v_+\|_2=\|v_-\|_2\), as required by lem:qmd-binary-likelihood-limit. Also thm:local-geometry gives
\[C_m(\delta_n)=c_1\delta_n^2\{1+o(1)\},\qquad c_1>0.	ag{24}\]
Suppose first that \(nC_m(\delta_n)	oho\in(0,\infty)\). Then \(\sqrt n\,\delta_n	o	au:=\sqrt{ho/c_1}\). Apply lem:qmd-binary-likelihood-limit with \(t_n=\delta_n\). For the log likelihood ratio \(\Lambda_n=\log(dP_{+,\delta_n}^n/dP_{-,\delta_n}^n)\), its limit under the plus law is \(N(	au^2c_2/2,	au^2c_2)\), and under the minus law it is \(N(-	au^2c_2/2,	au^2c_2)\). The likelihood-ratio rule constructed in lem:mirror-bayes-minimax-equality is an exact minimax equalizer. Since the limiting normal laws put no mass at zero, either error converges to
\[\Phi(-	au\sqrt{c_2}/2)=\Phi\left(- rac12\sqrt{ho c_2/c_1}ight).	ag{25}\]
If \(ho=0\), the total-variation conclusion of lem:qmd-binary-likelihood-limit applies. The equal-prior Bayes error is \(B_n=(1-\|P_{+,\delta_n}^n-P_{-,\delta_n}^n\|_{m TV})/2	o1/2\); the exact identity \(R_n=B_n\) from lem:mirror-bayes-minimax-equality and \(\Phi(0)=1/2\) prove the same formula.

Finally suppose \(nC_m(\delta_n)	o\infty\). Mirror symmetry and thm:local-geometry identify the one-observation affinity as \(A_n=\int\sqrt{p_{+,\delta_n}p_{-,\delta_n}}=e^{-C_\star(\delta_n)}\). Therefore
\[R_n(\delta_n)=B_n(\delta_n)\le rac12A_n^n= rac12e^{-nC_\star(\delta_n)}.\]
Since \(C_\star(\delta_n)/C_m(\delta_n)	o c_2/(8c_1)>0\), the right side tends to zero. This proves the entire elbow without any uniform lower envelope over mechanisms \(g\).

added_lemmas.statement: Along every sequence delta_n downarrow 0 with r_n=o(sqrt(C_m(delta_n))) and n C_m(delta_n)->infinity, any training-measurable two-fold score-Jacobian estimates satisfying ass:jacobian-estimability obey widehat Delta_n/delta_n^2 -> c_1 in P_{+,delta_n}^n-probability and widehat Delta_n/delta_n^2 -> -c_1 in P_{-,delta_n}^n-probability. Consequently max_{h in {+,-}} P_{h,delta_n}^n{D_n != h}->0.
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
For an evaluation fold \(I_{n,k}\), write \(\mathbb V_k(u)=|I_{n,k}|^{-1}\sum_{i\in I_{n,k}}(u_i- ar u_k)^2\). The score calculation in thm:local-geometry gives, under \(P_{+,\delta}\),
\[J_y=-b^{-1},\qquad J_x=-b^{-1}+\delta W_\delta,\qquad W_\delta=b^{-1}\{E g''(Z)-2\alpha g'(Z)-\delta g'(Z)^2\}.	ag{29}\]
Under \(P_{-,\delta}\), the roles of \(x\) and \(y\) are exchanged. Ass:exponential-envelope and Gaussian moments imply, for every sufficiently small fixed \(\delta_1>0\),
\[\sup_{|\delta|\le\delta_1}E|W_\delta|^4<\infty.	ag{30}\]
Because \(nC_m(\delta_n)	o\infty\) and \(C_m(\delta_n)	o0\), both fold sizes diverge. Chebyshev's inequality applied to the first two empirical moments therefore yields the triangular-array law of large numbers
\[\mathbb V_k(W_{\delta_n})=\operatorname{Var}(W_{\delta_n})+o_P(1)=c_1+o_P(1),	ag{31}\]
where the last equality is \(C_m(\delta_n)/\delta_n^2	o c_1\) from thm:local-geometry.

Let \(e_{j,k}=\widehat J_{j,k}-J_j\). Ass:jacobian-estimability makes the training-random norm \(\|e_{j,k}\|_{L^4(P_{h,\delta_n})}=O_P(r_n)\), uniformly over the finite set of \((h,j,k)\). Conditional on the training fold, the observations in \(I_{n,k}\) are independent of \(e_{j,k}\). Conditional Markov's inequality and \(\|e\|_2\le\|e\|_4\) give
\[|I_{n,k}|^{-1}\sum_{i\in I_{n,k}}e_{j,k}(X_i,Y_i)^2=O_P(r_n^2),\qquad \mathbb V_k(e_{j,k})=O_P(r_n^2).	ag{32}\]
For arbitrary real vectors \(u,e\), expansion of centered squares and Cauchy--Schwarz give the deterministic inequality
\[|\mathbb V_k(u+e)-\mathbb V_k(u)|\le2\{\mathbb V_k(u)\mathbb V_k(e)\}^{1/2}+\mathbb V_k(e).	ag{33}\]
Applying (33) to (29) shows that the estimated non-leaf variance is
\[\delta_n^2\{c_1+o_P(1)\}+O_P(|\delta_n|r_n+r_n^2),	ag{34}\]
whereas the estimated leaf variance is \(O_P(r_n^2)\). By thm:local-geometry, \(\sqrt{C_m(\delta_n)}\sim\sqrt{c_1}|\delta_n|\). The premise \(r_n=o(\sqrt{C_m(\delta_n)})\) is therefore exactly \(r_n=o(|\delta_n|)\), which makes both remainder terms in (34) \(o_P(\delta_n^2)\). Averaging over the two folds proves the asserted plus-law limit. Coordinate exchange proves the minus-law limit. Finally \(\widehat\sigma_n\vee n^{-1}>0\), so def:crossfitted-score-detector assigns the sign of \(\widehat\Delta_n\); the two probability limits imply maximal sign error tending to zero.
